claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for your help. I'm looking to claim for my two children. There's Alex, who's 16, and Blake, who's 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's lovely to meet you, and I'd be happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start with a few questions about your circumstances.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Now, do both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I'll need to ask a few more questions about each child's situation.

Let's start with Alex, who doesn't live with you. Could you tell me a bit more about Alex's living arrangement? For example:
- Who is Alex currently living with?
- Are you contributing towards Alex's upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation costs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent. I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep - I help out with things like clothes, school expenses, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. 

Regarding your contributions towards Alex's upkeep, would you say the amount you contribute is at least equal to the current Child Benefit rate (which is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child)?

Also, since Alex is 16, I need to ask: is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, a BTEC, or other similar qualifications)? Or have they left education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I contribute more than that amount - it's about £50 a week actually.

And yes, Alex is still in education - doing A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's really helpful, thank you.

Just one more question about Alex: is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex (for example, their other parent)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Now let me ask a couple of questions about Blake, who is 5 and lives with you.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I just want to make sure I understand correctly – you mentioned earlier that Blake lives with you, but now you've said Blake lives with their other parent who is claiming Child Benefit. Could you confirm where Blake primarily lives?

Also, are you contributing towards Blake's upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation costs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me clarify - Blake does live with me, but their other parent also lives here with us. So Blake's other parent lives with Blake and is already claiming the Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ah, that makes sense now – thank you for clarifying! So Blake lives with you and their other parent, and the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and Blake's other parent is already receiving it, you would not be able to make a separate claim for Blake while they are claiming.

However, if you and Blake's other parent wanted to change who receives the Child Benefit, you could agree between yourselves and contact the Child Benefit Office to make that switch.

Now I have enough information to assess your eligibility. Let me summarise:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### **Alex (age 16)** ✅ **Eligible**

You appear to be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:
- Although Alex lives with their other parent, you are contributing approximately £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the minimum requirement of £25.60 per week (the Child Benefit rate)
- Alex is 16 but remains in approved education (A-levels), so eligibility can continue
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

---

### **Blake (age 5)** ❌ **Not Eligible**

You are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any time
- Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake

However, if you and Blake's other parent would like to change who receives the Child Benefit, you can agree between yourselves and contact the Child Benefit Office to arrange the switch.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which means eligibility can continue until age 20 as long as they remain in approved education or training. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing approximately £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week. This meets the requirement of contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child. Additionally, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. However, Blake's other parent, who also lives in the same household, is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since someone else is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, the claimant cannot make a separate claim. If they wished to change who receives the benefit, they would need to agree with the other parent and contact the Child Benefit Office to make the switch.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is inquiring about Child Benefit for two children. For Alex (age 16), who lives with the other parent but for whom the claimant contributes over £50 per week towards upkeep and who remains in approved education, the claimant is eligible to claim as no one else is currently claiming. For Blake (age 5), who lives with both the claimant and Blake's other parent, the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit, so the claimant cannot make a separate claim for the same child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 years old and currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which means eligibility can continue until age 20 as long as they remain in approved education or training. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing approximately £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week. This meets the requirement of contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child. Additionally, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 5 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. However, Blake's other parent, who also lives in the same household, is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since someone else is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, the claimant cannot make a separate claim. If they wished to change who receives the benefit, they would need to agree with the other parent and contact the Child Benefit Office to make the switch.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is inquiring about Child Benefit for two children. For Alex (age 16), who lives with the other parent but for whom the claimant contributes over £50 per week towards upkeep and who remains in approved education, the claimant is eligible to claim as no one else is currently claiming. For Blake (age 5), who lives with both the claimant and Blake's other parent, the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit, so the claimant cannot make a separate claim for the same child.
